meta.section: Endpoint Management — Windows (Phase 5 of 7: Security hardening config profiles / Settings Catalog)
meta.source: Microsoft Learn (learn.microsoft.com/intune + windows/security) — portal paths and Settings Catalog categories grounded via the Microsoft Learn MCP on 2026-07-03.
generalNotes: All 13 are Windows device configuration profiles, almost all built in the Settings Catalog: Intune admin center > Devices > Configuration > Create > New policy > Platform = Windows 10 and later > Profile type = Settings catalog. Add the settings for the named category, then assign to the Windows device group.
Most are LOW-impact hardening and invisible to users; the exception (WHfB, MEDIUM) changes how people sign in. A few (Credential Guard/VBS) require a device reboot to take effect.

Many of these overlap with Intune's built-in Security Baseline for Windows — you can source them from the baseline instead of hand-building profiles, but keep one authoritative source per setting to avoid conflicts.
Validate on a pilot device: Devices > Configuration > (policy) > Per setting status should show Succeeded, not Error, before broadening assignment.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog

requiredSettings.label: Intent
requiredSettings.value: ~53 OS hardening settings — Structured Exception Handling (SEHOP) protection, RPC security, Remote Assistance restrictions, and related system controls

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Add the baseline hardening settings (SEHOP, RPC hardening, disable Remote Assistance, etc.) and assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact / invisible — general OS-level attack-surface hardening.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog (Windows Security / Defender Security Center)

requiredSettings.label: Intent
requiredSettings.value: Control which Windows Security app panels are visible; hide/lock areas users shouldn't change; set the org contact info

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Add Windows Security app (Defender Security Center) settings to show/hide panels and set org support info; assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact — cosmetic/guard-rail control of the Windows Security UI.

requiredSettings.label: Windows SmartScreen
requiredSettings.value: Enable SmartScreen for apps/files = On; block bypass of warnings (PreventOverride) = On

requiredSettings.label: Edge SmartScreen
requiredSettings.value: Microsoft Edge > SmartScreen settings > Configure Microsoft Defender SmartScreen = Enabled (and block PUA)

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Enable SmartScreen for Windows (apps/files, prevent override) and for Microsoft Edge (Configure Microsoft Defender SmartScreen + block potentially unwanted apps).
Assign to the Windows device group. Note: LOW impact — warns/blocks known-malicious downloads and sites.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog — category Device Guard

requiredSettings.label: Credential Guard (LSA protection)
requiredSettings.value: Enabled with UEFI lock (or 'without lock' if you need to be able to disable remotely)

requiredSettings.label: Virtualization Based Security
requiredSettings.value: Turn On VBS = Enabled; Secure Boot with DMA protection (enabling Credential Guard turns these on)

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Under Device Guard set Credential Guard = Enabled with UEFI lock and Turn On Virtualization Based Security = Enabled (VBS + Secure Boot/DMA come along with it).
Assign to the Windows device group. A reboot is required for these to take effect.
Note: LOW impact / invisible but reboot-gated — kernel/credential isolation against pass-the-hash and memory attacks. 'Enabled with UEFI lock' can't be turned off remotely, so choose deliberately.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog

requiredSettings.label: Config Refresh cadence
requiredSettings.value: Enabled with a periodic refresh (e.g. every 90 minutes) so local tampering/drift is re-corrected to policy

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Enable Config Refresh and set the refresh cadence; assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact / invisible — periodically re-applies MDM policy to undo configuration drift between normal check-ins.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog (category Windows Hello for Business) or Endpoint security > Account protection profile

requiredSettings.label: Use Windows Hello for Business
requiredSettings.value: Enabled (true)

requiredSettings.label: Require security device (TPM)
requiredSettings.value: True

requiredSettings.label: PIN policy
requiredSettings.value: Minimum PIN length per baseline; allow biometric gestures (fingerprint/face)

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog (or Endpoint security > Account protection).
Set Use Windows Hello For Business = true, Require Security Device = true, minimum PIN length, and allow biometrics.
Assign to the Windows device/user group. (A tenant-wide WHfB policy also exists at Devices > Windows > Windows Enrollment — use one authoritative source to avoid PIN-policy conflicts.)
Note (MEDIUM impact): users set up a PIN/biometric sign-in in place of a password — a visible, one-time enrollment step; brief users so they aren't surprised at next sign-in.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog (Internet Explorer / legacy)

requiredSettings.label: Intent
requiredSettings.value: Harden the legacy IE engine — disable insecure features / restrict zones (IE11 is retired; Edge IE mode is the modern path)

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Apply the IE hardening settings from the baseline; assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact — legacy-browser hardening; most orgs use Edge IE mode for any remaining IE-dependent sites.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog (Printers / Print security)

requiredSettings.label: Intent
requiredSettings.value: Harden printing — e.g. restrict Point-and-Print to admins, block spooler-driven privilege escalation (PrintNightmare mitigations)

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Apply the print-security settings (Point-and-Print restrictions, package-point-and-print, spooler hardening) per the baseline; assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact — mitigates print-spooler attack vectors; verify legitimate network printers still install for users.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog (Auditing / Advanced Audit Policy)

requiredSettings.label: Intent
requiredSettings.value: Turn on the baseline Windows audit categories (logon/account, object access, policy change, etc.) and event-log sizing for investigation readiness

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Enable the baseline advanced-audit categories and set event-log retention/size; assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact / invisible — improves forensic/audit coverage; pairs with WUFB reports and MDE for visibility.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog (Device Lock / Local Policies Security Options)

requiredSettings.label: Intent
requiredSettings.value: Auto-lock/inactivity timeout, require password on resume, hide last signed-in user, limit lock-screen exposure

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Set the machine inactivity limit (auto-lock), require password on resume/screensaver, and the lock-screen hardening options; assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact — devices lock after inactivity and prompt for credentials on resume.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog (User Rights Assignment)

requiredSettings.label: Intent
requiredSettings.value: Constrain privileged rights — who can log on locally / over RDP, deny network logon to local accounts, restrict 'debug programs' / 'take ownership', etc.

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Apply the baseline User Rights Assignments (limit sensitive privileges to the intended accounts/groups); assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact if scoped correctly — but test carefully, since over-tightening a right (e.g. 'Allow log on locally') can lock out legitimate service/admin accounts.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog (Local Policies / Security Options)

requiredSettings.label: Intent
requiredSettings.value: Local security options — account lockout thresholds, audit policy, network access (anonymous SID/name translation), UAC behavior, LSA hardening

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Apply the baseline Local Policies / Security Options (lockout, UAC, network access restrictions, LSA); assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact / invisible — device-local security posture aligned to the baseline.

requiredSettings.label: Profile type
requiredSettings.value: Settings catalog (Network / SMB / Authentication)

requiredSettings.label: Intent
requiredSettings.value: Network protocol hardening — require SMB signing, disable SMBv1, LDAP signing/channel binding, restrict NTLM, disable insecure guest logons

steps: Devices > Configuration > Create > Windows 10 and later > Settings catalog.
Apply the network-security settings (SMB signing, no SMBv1, LDAP signing, NTLM restrictions, no insecure guest auth); assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact for modern environments — but if any legacy device/app relies on SMBv1 or unsigned SMB, test first.
